passage.text:
The University Photography Club
The University Photography Club invites you to a weekend of learning and creativity at the
Student Center, Meeting Hall B. This annual event is perfect for beginners and experienced
photographers alike. Enjoy hands-on sessions, expert tips, and a chance to showcase your
work.
Saturday, October 16
• 9:00-10:30: Registration and Welcome Coffee
• 10:30-12:00: Introduction to Photography-basics of camera settings, composition, and
lighting
• 12:00-1:30: Lunch break (lunch provided)
• 1:30-3:00: Portrait Photography-techniques for stunning portraits
• 3:00-4:30: Landscape Photography-tips for nature and outdoor shots
Sunday, October 17
• 9:00-10:30: Photo Editing Workshop-enhance images with editing software
• 10:30-12:00: Street Photography-capture candid urban moments
• 12:00-1:30: Lunch break (lunch provided)
• 1:30-3:00: Advanced Techniques-creative methods for unique shots
• 3:00-4:30: Juried Photo Contest-submit your best photos for prizes
Register at university.edu/photoclub/register.
Contact 555-1234 or info@photoclub.university.edu. for any queries.
We look forward to seeing you there and capturing beautiful moments together!

questions.directive: Read the passage and choose the best answer.
questions.prompt: All of the following contributed to the status of Carthage as a wealthy and powerful city-state EXCEPT

passage.text: Carthage, founded by Phoenician merchants around 814 B.C.E., became a powerful city-state due to its strategic location on the Mediterranean. This position allowed Carthage to establish a vast trade network that extended across the Mediterranean and into Africa, Europe, and Asia. Carthaginian traders dealt in goods such as silver, gold, tin, and textiles. The Carthaginians excelled in maritime technology, constructing advanced ships for long-distance journeys and bulky cargo transport. They developed efficient trade routes and set up colonies, expanding their economic reach. Their prowess in navigation and trade transformed Carthage into one of the wealthiest cities in the ancient world. Carthage's wealth was also built on innovations in agriculture. Carthaginian farmers employed advanced irrigation techniques and crop rotation, leading to abundant harvests that supported both local consumption and export. However, Carthage's rise did not come without challenges. Its growing wealth and dominance in trade led to rivalry with Rome. The Punic Wars, a series of three wars fought against Rome from 264 to 146 B.C.E., were rooted in competition for control of Mediterranean trade routes. Despite initial successes, Carthage was ultimately defeated. The final blow came when the Romans launched a brutal siege on Carthage, culminating in the city's destruction.

options.text: its trade network across three continents

Carthage, founded by Phoenician merchants around 814 B.C.E., became a powerful city-state due to its strategic location on the Mediterranean. This position allowed Carthage to establish a vast trade network that extended across the Mediterranean and into Africa, Europe, and Asia. Carthaginian traders dealt in goods such as silver, gold, tin, and textiles. The Carthaginians excelled in maritime technology, constructing advanced ships for long-distance journeys and bulky cargo transport. They developed efficient trade routes and set up colonies, expanding their economic reach. Their prowess in navigation and trade transformed Carthage into one of the wealthiest cities in the ancient world. Carthage's wealth was also built on innovations in agriculture. Carthaginian farmers employed advanced irrigation techniques and crop rotation, leading to abundant harvests that supported both local consumption and export. However, Carthage's rise did not come without challenges. Its growing wealth and dominance in trade led to rivalry with Rome. The Punic Wars, a series of three wars fought against Rome from 264 to 146 B.C.E., were rooted in competition for control of Mediterranean trade routes. Despite initial successes, Carthage was ultimately defeated. The final blow came when the Romans launched a brutal siege on Carthage, culminating in the city's destruction.

questions.directive: Read the passage and choose the best answer.
questions.prompt: The word "promoting" in the passage is closest in meaning to

passage.text: Advances in traumatology have highlighted the potential of electromagnetic fields (EMFs) in promoting bone healing. EMFs, invisible areas of energy produced by electrically charged objects, are recognized for their therapeutic benefits. Researchers have found that specific EMF frequencies can stimulate cellular activity, enhancing bone repair. Unlike traditional treatments such as metal implants and bone grafts, EMF therapy is non-invasive and produces few side effects. In clinical studies, patients with non-healing fractures exposed to EMFs showed significant improvement. One study showed that EMFs reduced recovery time by accelerating healing by up to 50 percent, a significant difference for patients with conditions that impede natural healing. Multiple mechanisms driven by EMFs have been identified as contributing to bone healing, yet the complete picture is not fully understood. Some scientists believe that EMFs enhance the production of substances known as growth factors, which include essential proteins for cell growth and repair. Others note that EMFs improve blood circulation to the damaged area, facilitating nutrient delivery and waste removal. Researchers are uncertain of how the mechanisms interact, the optimal parameters (frequency, intensity, duration) for therapies, and the relation between the amount of a treatment and the magnitude of the effect it produces.

Researchers have found that specific EMF frequencies can stimulate cellular activity, enhancing bone repair. Unlike traditional treatments such as metal implants and bone grafts, EMF therapy is non-invasive and produces few side effects. Multiple mechanisms driven by EMFs have been identified as contributing to bone healing, yet the complete picture is not fully understood.
